title: Christian Certainty
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Christian Certainty - Pastor Tito Lyro - 1 John 1.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Value of Systematic Bible Study and the Letter of 1 John
subpoints: Systematic study allows us to preach whatever the text says regardless of who may be offended
1 John is the shortest and simplest in Greek of all New Testament letters — only 5 chapters, 105 verses, 2141 words
John does not follow a linear argument but a spiral progression, revisiting themes as he moves toward the conclusion
John loved these people deeply — he calls them 'my little children' throughout the letter

outline.title: Who Wrote 1 John and What Do We Know About Him?
subpoints: The letter has no introduction or signature, but internal evidence strongly confirms the apostle John as author
John was likely a teenager when called by Christ; he calls himself 'the disciple whom Jesus loved' in his Gospel
He was the only apostle to die a natural death, exiled to Patmos, and later became pastor of the Ephesian church
He may have been a cousin of Jesus through Salome; he succeeded Paul and Timothy as pastor at Ephesus

outline.title: To Whom Was 1 John Written and When?
subpoints: The audience is uncertain — possibly the churches of the Lycus Valley or the church at Ephesus
1 John was likely written in the early AD 90s — around the same time Hipparchus calculated the value of pi
The absence of Old Testament quotations (only one, to Cain in 3:12) suggests a predominantly Gentile audience
Divine inspiration did not make the biblical authors robots — God used their personalities, histories, and intentions

outline.title: Why Was 1 John Written? The Problem of False Teachers
subpoints: Most of the New Testament arose because there were troubles in the church — 1 John is no exception
Paul warned the Ephesian elders in Acts 20 that savage wolves would come from both outside and inside the flock
False teachers taught Docetism: Jesus did not come in the flesh — either he was a ghost or the Christ left before the crucifixion
These antichrists were once members of the church who left over doctrine and returned as false prophets

outline.title: The False Teachers' Ethics: Faith Without Obedience
subpoints: The false teachers also claimed that faith in Christ did not need to result in obedience
John addresses this through the 'if' claims of chapter 1: if we say we have no sin, we are liars
The false teachers also denied the need to love the brethren — John calls this an impossibility
John wanted to protect his beloved people, to establish them in faith and life, and to make their joy complete

outline.title: The Main Message: Three Objective Tests of Saving Faith
subpoints: The purpose of 1 John: 'that you may know that you have eternal life' — assurance through objective standards, not secret knowledge
Test 1: What do you believe concerning Christ? — right belief about his person and work (1 John 5:10)
Test 2: Is your life marked by obedience to God's Word? — not perfection but the direction and movie of your life (1 John 2:3-6)
Test 3: Do you love the brethren? — not abstract brethren far away but the people around you who have the opportunity to annoy you

summary:
In this introductory sermon to a series on 1 John, Pastor Tito Lyro covers the background and main message of the epistle rather than expounding the text verse by verse. He opens with a defense of systematic biblical preaching — citing the famous story of Karl Barth preaching love for Jews in 1939 Germany and saying, simply, 'It was in the text' — arguing that going through books of the Bible commits the preacher to say whatever the text says regardless of who may be offended.

The sermon surveys the key introductory questions. On authorship: though 1 John contains no name, the internal evidence — including the eyewitness language of verses 1-4 and the shared vocabulary with the Gospel of John — virtually confirms the apostle John as author. John was likely a teenager when called, was probably a blood relative of Jesus, called himself 'the disciple whom Jesus loved,' and was the only apostle to die a natural death. He eventually became pastor of the church at Ephesus, succeeding Paul and Timothy. On dating: the letter was probably written in the early AD 90s. On audience: the absence of Old Testament quotations (only one reference to Cain in chapter 3) suggests a predominantly Gentile readership, possibly the churches of Asia Minor.

The occasion for the letter is false teaching. Paul had warned the Ephesian elders in Acts 20 that wolves would come from both outside and inside the flock, and by the time John wrote, that prophecy had come true. The false teachers — called deceivers, false prophets, and antichrists throughout the letter — denied the incarnation (Docetism), claiming either that Jesus was a phantom or that the divine Christ left before the crucifixion. They also taught that faith in Christ required no obedience and no love for the brethren. John wrote to protect his beloved church.

The sermon closes with the main message: 1 John gives three objective tests of saving faith that yield assurance — not the subjective secret knowledge the false teachers offered, but visible markers any believer can examine. Do you believe what the Bible teaches about Christ? Is the direction of your life marked by growing obedience to God's word? And do you love the brethren — not missionaries in distant lands but the people around you who have every opportunity to annoy you?
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: 1 John 1

If you're able, remain standing and turn to the first epistle of John. 1 John, we need to read the first chapter. 1 John chapter 1, starting at verse 1. This is the word of our Lord.

*That which was from the beginning, which we have heard, which we have seen with our eyes, which we have looked upon, and our hands have handled, concerning the word of life — the life was manifested, and we have seen and bear witness and declare to you that eternal life which was with the Father and was manifested to us. That which we have seen and heard we declare to you, that you also may have fellowship with us; and truly our fellowship is with the Father and with his Son Jesus Christ. And these things we write to you that your joy may be full. This is the message which we have heard from him and declared to you, that God is light and in him is no darkness at all. If we say that we have fellowship with him, and walk in darkness, we lie and do not practice the truth. But if we walk in the light as he is in the light, we have fellowship with one another, and the blood of Jesus Christ his Son cleanses us from all sin. If we say that we have no sin, we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us. If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness. If we say that we have not sinned, we make him a liar, and his word is not in us.*

This is the word of our Lord. Let us pray.

*Father, we pray that your Spirit be present with us as we consider this book this morning. For asking in Jesus name. Amen.*

Please be seated.

## The Value of Systematic Bible Study

There is a great value in systematically studying the Word of God. The practice of going through book by book of the Bible allows us to consider the whole counsel of God. It also gives us the opportunity to address whatever the text is saying.

I'll tell you about Karl Barth. Karl Barth was not a good theologian in the orthodox sense of the word. One time he was preaching on John 3:16 and said that God loved the world and the world includes Jews — we must love Jews too. He was preaching that in 1939 Germany, and people got up leaving mad and protesting out the door. The press came and talked to him and asked why he said that — why did you upset your members? Barth's response was: it was in the text. That's it.

Preaching through the books of the Bible allows us to preach what the text is saying. The preacher doesn't have to worry about who he may be offending — it's just saying what the text is saying. It's just the next passage and you preach on that.

Well, today we start a study on the first epistle of John. I'm not sure how long the series will go — I'm learning not to make false predictions about the length of series. 1 John is a short epistle — only five chapters, 105 verses, 2141 words in the original language. The Greek behind the English text we have in front of us is the easiest, simplest of the New Testament. When you study Greek, this is the first book you translate because it's an easy book to do so. And yet it's a book whose theology is deep and complex — that's been stated about it throughout the centuries.

It is not easy to outline 1 John because John is not following a linear argument. He shapes his book in a spiral progression where he keeps revisiting arguments as he moves to the conclusion. He has a beginning and end but the argument comes around and around, revisiting arguments as he goes through the book. As we go through the series you're going to notice that we'll talk about something in chapter 1 and then when we come to chapter 2 we go back to what we were talking about in chapter 1 — that's how John designed this book.

The goal for today is to consider general things about the letter that will help us understand the book as a whole — what scholars refer to as introductory material. Questions of who wrote it, why was it written, when was it written, and what's the main message.

## Who Wrote 1 John?

You might say: well, it says right there on top of my Bible, 'the First Epistle of John.' But if you read the text, it doesn't tell us who wrote it — there's no introduction, no name on it. Yet there is very little doubt that the person who wrote 1 John is also the person who wrote the fourth Gospel, and the same person who wrote 2 and 3 John. And it's well established that the fourth Gospel is written by the apostle John.

Notice right off the bat in verses 1 through 4 how the language points to the author being an eyewitness of Jesus' earthly ministry. He talks about that which we have heard, which we have seen with our eyes, which we have looked upon, which our hands have handled. This is the indication of an eyewitness — intimate with the ministry of Jesus.

We know that John was the youngest of all the disciples. It is plausible that he was a teenager when he was called to be a follower of Jesus Christ. He was a close friend of Jesus alongside his brother James and Peter — one of the three men invited to witness the transfiguration, one of the three men invited to pray with Jesus in the garden the night before he was crucified.

And remember his favorite title for himself in the Gospel of John — the disciple whom Jesus loved. If you read the Gospel of John, the name John is only used for John the Baptist. When John refers to himself, he calls himself the disciple whom Jesus loved.

There is also a strong tradition that Salome, his mother, was Mary's sister — making John and Jesus first cousins. So they would have known each other for a while before John became a disciple.

As far as we know, John was the only disciple, the only apostle, to die a natural death rather than as a martyr. Church tradition tells us that he died at almost 100 years old at the end of the first century. He was exiled to Patmos, the island off modern-day Turkey, during the reign of Domitian — exiled for the word of God and the testimony of Jesus Christ. He eventually became the pastor of the Ephesian church, ministering there until his death around AD 98.

Think about it — who was the first pastor of the Ephesian church? The apostle Paul. Then Aquila became pastor according to Acts 18. Then Timothy becomes the pastor. And then the apostle John. Timothy here is not exactly encouraged — he seems to struggle with fear of man. It didn't help that he was the pastor sandwiched between the apostle Paul and the apostle John. You never want to follow a well-known guy.

## To Whom Was 1 John Written?

The original audience is impossible to know with certainty because it doesn't list them here. The epistle lacks the usual letter introduction — there are no greetings in it, and there's no conclusion either. If you look at the very last verse — 'keep yourselves from idols' — that's how the epistle ends. Even Galatians, where Paul skips all the niceties, still says 'Paul' at the start. But in 1 John, John just goes straight to the point.

So we don't know with certainty to whom he is writing. It is clear, however, that he wrote this letter to a group of people who were struggling to hold together all the apostolic teaching concerning the person of Jesus Christ. And it seems like his audience was struggling a little to put all that teaching together because some false teachers had come in and were leading them astray.

One thing that's very obvious when you read 1 John is that John loved these people. Throughout the letter he calls them 'my little children' — and he's not just talking to the children's Sunday school department; he's talking to the whole church.

Also notice that there's no quotation or reference to the Old Testament except for one — in chapter 3 verse 12: 'not as Cain who was of the wicked one and murdered his brother.' Just that one reference. This would lead us to think that he's writing to a predominantly Gentile audience, not as familiar perhaps with the Old Testament.

Putting all this together, John may have written this epistle from Ephesus to the churches in the Lycus Valley — the churches listed in the book of Revelation, the seven churches — or perhaps he was traveling and heard of these false teachers and wrote back to the Christians at Ephesus as a love letter to explain further who Christ is and what he's done for them.

## When Was 1 John Written?

The argument for the dating of 1 John is very convoluted, but the conclusion is that 1 John was likely written in the early AD 90s. Incidentally, when the book of 1 John is being written, Hipparchus calculates for the first time the value of pi. Not the banana cream pie that Lewis brought to church for lunch today — but the mathematical 3.1416 that allows us to put a man on the moon. History keeps moving.

## Why Was 1 John Written? The Problem of False Teachers

Now this is an important question, because knowing why a book was written helps us interpret what's in it. Divine inspiration does not deny human intent. The authors of the Bible are not robots who were zapped and then the books came out. The divine inspiration used everything about the author's life to assure that the final product was the inspired and inerrant word of God. So John's words are also God's words — exactly what God wanted.

The primary impetus for the writing of this letter was that John became aware of false teachers who had come into the church and were threatening her well-being. Verse 2:26: 'These things I have written to you concerning those who try to deceive you.' And 3:7: 'Little children, let no one deceive you.'

Paul had warned about this. In Acts 20:29-30, Paul says: for I know this, that after my departure savage wolves will come in among you, not sparing the flock. Also from among yourselves men will rise up, speaking perverse things, to draw away the disciples after themselves.

And he warned Timothy similarly in 2 Timothy 3:1-7: know this, that in the last days perilous times will come. Men will be lovers of themselves, lovers of money, boasters, proud, blasphemers, disobedient to parents, unthankful, unholy — having a form of godliness but denying its power.

John then realized that these prophetic warnings came true and that's why he writes 1 John. He uses three expressions to refer to these wicked false teachers: in chapter 4 verse 1 he calls them false prophets; in 2 John verse 7 he calls them deceivers; and throughout 1 John and 2 John he calls them antichrists.

John doesn't pull back. The idea of not calling people what they need to be called is a fairly new thing as far as preaching goes. If you read the Bible and look through church history, that has not been the case — people have been willing to name names and call them what the Bible calls them.

By the end of the first century, heretics abounded. John says in 2:18: 'little children, it is the last hour; and as you have heard that the antichrist is coming, even now many antichrists have come, by which we know that it is the last hour.' The New Testament writers believed that the whole time from Christ's resurrection until his coming is 'the last hour,' and antichrists would come as types of that final antichrist before the Lord's return.

The worst thing was that these antichrists had come from among the people of God. Look at 2:19: 'they went out from us, but they were not of us; for if they had been of us, they would have continued with us; but they went out that they might be made manifest, that none of them were of us.'

These false teachers were at one time members of the church who left on their own accord for doctrinal reasons and came back later as false prophets trying to lead the members astray. And that's even more powerful — because we're more likely to believe somebody who comes from among us than somebody from 'out there.'

One example from the first century we have good records on is a man named Cerinthus. Several church fathers tell us about this man who lived in Ephesus. Irenaeus, a disciple of Polycarp who was a disciple of John, tells this funny story: 'John, the disciple of the Lord, going to bathe at Ephesus and perceiving Cerinthus within, rushed out of the bathhouse without bathing, exclaiming: let us fly, lest even the bathhouse fall down, because Cerinthus, the enemy of truth, is within.' Not a lot of willingness to associate with false teachers there.

### The Docetic Heresy

These false teachers were teaching that Jesus did not live in the flesh. They were denying the incarnation. Look at 4:1-3: 'beloved, do not believe every spirit, but test the spirits, whether they are of God; because many false prophets have gone out into the world. By this you know the Spirit of God: every spirit that confesses that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh is of God, and every spirit that does not confess that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh is not of God. And this is the spirit of the antichrist.'

These teachers were what theology calls Docetists — they denied that Jesus had a real body. Either by saying he was a hologram, an apparition, a ghost-like thing that couldn't be touched (thus John in chapter 1 emphasizes: that which our hands have handled) — or that Jesus was a man and at his baptism the divine Christ came upon him, but before the crucifixion the Christ left, so only the man Jesus died.

Interestingly, the early church struggled not with the divinity of Christ but with the humanity of Christ. They had a hard time understanding how God could become man. Today the church tends to have the opposite problem.

### Faith Without Ethics

The false teachers also claimed that faith in Christ didn't have to result in obedience. You can come to faith in Christ and that doesn't have to impact the way you live your life. You can see this in the 'if' claims of chapter 1 verses 6 through 10 — all those 'if you say this, then conclude that' statements. For example, if you say you have no sin, you are a liar. And you can see it in the exhortations to love the brethren — these false teachers were saying you can believe in Jesus and not have to love the brethren. John says that's an impossibility.

Look at 2:5-6: 'whoever keeps his word, truly the love of God is perfected in him. By this we know that we are in him. He who says he abides in him ought himself also to walk just as he walked.' And in chapter 3 verse 10: 'if you say that you love God but you hate your brother, you are a liar — you don't love God, because it's impossible to love God without loving the brethren around you.'

## The Main Message: Three Objective Tests of Saving Faith

Ultimately John wrote this letter to protect the church he loved. His thought, as one commentator says, was to protect his readers, his beloved children, and to establish them in their Christian faith and life. He defines his own purpose in writing as being to make their joy complete, so that they will not sin, and so that they may know that they have eternal life — joy, holiness, assurance. These are the Christian qualities the pastor desires to see in his flock.

The main message of this letter is that Christian assurance of faith comes through objective standards. Look at chapter 5 verse 13: 'these things I have written to you who believe in the name of the Son of God, that you may know that you have eternal life.' That's different from the Gospel of John, which says 'I am writing this so that you may have eternal life.' This letter says: so that you may know that you have eternal life — assurance. And he says that assurance comes not from some subjective secret knowledge that the false teachers were offering, but from objective tests that you can see present in your life.

John says there are three simple tests for you to know if you truly are saved.

**Test one:** What do you believe concerning Christ? Do you believe what the Bible teaches concerning the person and work of Christ? 1 John 5:10: 'he who believes in the Son of God has the witness in himself; he who does not believe God has made him a liar, because he has not believed the testimony that God has given of his Son.' Not how you feel about it — but do you believe that what the Bible says concerning Jesus is true?

**Test two:** Is your life marked by obedience to the Word of God? From chapter 2 verses 3 through 6: 'by this we know that we know him, if we keep his commandments. He who says "I know him" and does not keep his commandments is a liar, and the truth is not in him.' Not perfection — but is the movie of your Christian life a movie of growth in holiness? Is it a movie that shows somebody who is striving by God's grace to obey what the Bible says?

**Test three:** Do you love the brethren? You may believe everything right about Christ. You may strive in your personal life to obey God. But do you love the brethren? And here, these are not faceless brethren far away — not the missionaries in Djibouti or some place you've never heard of that we pray over permanently. The brethren that John mentions here are those close to you — who have the opportunity to annoy you. Do you love the people in front of you, behind you, next to you?

Do you believe what the Bible says concerning Christ? Is your life a demonstration of a desire to obey God by his grace? And do you love the brethren? This is how we know that we have eternal life — objectively listed in the book of 1 John. And by God's grace we're going to see that over the next however many sermons we preach through 1 John.

*Father, thank you for this epistle. We pray that as we journey through it you would greatly bless us with increased knowledge of Jesus Christ, increased practice of your word, and increased love for the brethren. We pray that you'll be working mightily through this series in all of us, for asking in Jesus name. Amen.*
